# Inkwell

Inkwell is a Markdown editor and renderer for React. The editor content model is
the Markdown source string; Markdown syntax is part of the content.

## Install

```sh
pnpm add @railway/inkwell
```

Import styles once:

```tsx
import "@railway/inkwell/styles.css";
```

## Editor

Use the component API:

```tsx
import { InkwellEditor } from "@railway/inkwell";
import { useState } from "react";

function App() {
  const [content, setContent] = useState("# Hello **world**");

  return <InkwellEditor content={content} onChange={setContent} />;
}
```

`onChange` receives the updated Markdown source string.

Use a ref for imperative actions:

```tsx
import { type InkwellEditorHandle, InkwellEditor } from "@railway/inkwell";
import { useRef } from "react";

const editorRef = useRef<InkwellEditorHandle>(null);

<InkwellEditor ref={editorRef} content={content} onChange={setContent} />;

editorRef.current?.getState().content;
editorRef.current?.focus({ at: "end" });
editorRef.current?.setContent("Replacement");
editorRef.current?.insertContent(" **more**");
editorRef.current?.clear();
```

`setContent()` and `clear()` do not call `onChange`. `insertContent()` behaves
like an edit and flows through normal change handling.

## Styling

Use slot styling:

```tsx
<InkwellEditor
  content={content}
  onChange={setContent}
  className="editor-shell"
  classNames={{ editor: "editor-surface" }}
  styles={{ editor: { minHeight: 180 } }}
/>;
```

`className` is an alias for `classNames.root`. Do not use a top-level `style`
prop; use `styles.root` or `styles.editor`.

The bundled stylesheet ships no `min-height`, `max-height`, or `height`
opinion on `.inkwell-editor` — container sizing is a consumer decision.
Set the size you want via `styles.editor` or your own CSS class.

Every visual-chrome default (color, background, border, padding,
typography) across the editor surface, plugins, and renderer is wrapped
in `:where()` so any single-class consumer rule wins by specificity
tie-break — no `!important` or descendant scoping. Tailwind utilities on
`classNames.editor` win automatically; restyling a rendered tag via
`<InkwellRenderer components={...} />` with a single class on the
wrapped element also wins. Layout-critical rules (positioning of the
wrapper, bubble menu, picker popup, copy button, and the picker list's
structural `max-height`) deliberately keep their normal specificity so
consumer classes can't silently break geometry. For sweeping color
changes, override the CSS custom properties (`--inkwell-accent`,
`--inkwell-text`, `--inkwell-border`, etc.) on the wrapper. The token
definitions themselves — including the `prefers-color-scheme: dark`
block — are also wrapped in `:where()`, so apps that drive light/dark
via a root class (e.g. `:root.dark .inkwell-renderer { ... }`) can
remap tokens at single-class specificity without doubled-class hacks.

## Features

Use `features` to disable Markdown recognition:

```tsx
<InkwellEditor
  content={content}
  onChange={setContent}
  features={{
    codeBlocks: false,
    headings: { h4: false, h5: false, h6: false },
  }}
/>;
```

Available features are `headings`, `blockquotes`, `codeBlocks`, and `images`.

## Renderer

```tsx
import { InkwellRenderer } from "@railway/inkwell";

<InkwellRenderer content="# Hello **world**" />;
```

Renderer utilities:

```tsx
import { htmlToMarkdown, parseMarkdown } from "@railway/inkwell";

const reactNodes = parseMarkdown(content, {
  components,
  rehypePlugins: [[rehypePlugin, ...options]],
  mentions,
});
const markdown = htmlToMarkdown(html);
```

`RehypePluginConfig` accepts a plugin function or `[plugin, ...options]`.

## Plugins

Built-in plugin factories include:

- `createAttachmentsPlugin` — `onUpload(file)` returns `string | { url: string; alt?: string }`. Image files are inserted inline; non-image files surface through optional `onAttachmentAdd(attachment)` so the consumer can track them as message-level state. Non-image files with no `onAttachmentAdd` pass through to default paste/drop. Pass `ref?: RefObject<AttachmentsHandle | null>` to receive an imperative `upload(files)` method (populated on mount, nulled on unmount) for click-to-attach buttons.
- `createBubbleMenuPlugin` — options type is `BubbleMenuOptions`
- `createCompletionsPlugin` — options type is `CompletionsPluginOptions`
- `createEmojiPlugin` — custom item generics work with `emojis` or `search`
- `createMentionsPlugin`
- `createSlashCommandsPlugin` — commands use one optional `arg?: SlashCommandArg`
- `createSnippetsPlugin`

Example:

```tsx
const snippets = createSnippetsPlugin({
  trigger: "[",
  snippets: [{ title: "Greeting", content: "Hello **world**" }],
});

<InkwellEditor content={content} onChange={setContent} plugins={[snippets]} />;
```

`characterLimit` is a soft budget. Typing past it is allowed — the editor
does not block input or truncate content. When a limit is set, the editor
renders a `count / limit` readout overlaying the top-right of the wrapper
once the count reaches 80% of the limit, inclusive (below that, the
readout stays hidden). The readout sits on a solid surface background and
is absolutely positioned, so it visually layers above wrapped text without
shifting content. The readout turns red when over the limit, the wrapper
picks up `.inkwell-editor-has-character-limit` whenever a limit is
configured, and gains `.inkwell-editor-over-limit` while
`characterCount > characterLimit` (which paints a soft red halo on the
editor surface — intentionally muted, not a hard ring). The `overLimit`
field on `InkwellEditorState` reflects the same condition.

```tsx
<InkwellEditor
  content={content}
  onChange={setContent}
  characterLimit={280}
/>;
```

## Custom plugin shape

```tsx
interface InkwellPlugin {
  name: string;
  activation?:
    | { type: "always" }
    | { type: "trigger"; key: string }
    | { type: "manual" };
  render?: (props: PluginRenderProps) => ReactNode;
  getPlaceholder?: (
    editor: InkwellPluginEditor,
  ) => string | InkwellPluginPlaceholder | null;
  onEditorChange?: (editor: InkwellPluginEditor) => void;
  shouldTrigger?: (
    event: React.KeyboardEvent,
    ctx: PluginKeyDownContext,
  ) => boolean;
  onKeyDown?: (event: React.KeyboardEvent, ctx: PluginKeyDownContext) => void;
  onActiveKeyDown?: (
    event: React.KeyboardEvent,
    ctx: PluginKeyDownContext,
  ) => false | void;
  onInsertData?: (
    data: DataTransfer,
    ctx: PluginInsertDataContext,
  ) => boolean | void;
  setup?: (editor: InkwellPluginEditor) => void | (() => void);
}
```

Use `ctx.activate()` and `ctx.dismiss()` for manual activation. Plugins receive a
narrow `InkwellPluginEditor` controller for editor actions.

Render props include `position` (wrapper-relative caret coords with a 4px gap
below the caret, intended as the popup's default top-left anchor) and
`cursorRect` (`{ top, bottom, left }`, also wrapper-relative). Built-in pickers
flip above the caret when the popup would clip the editor wrapper's bottom or
the viewport bottom (whichever is more restrictive), and shift left when it
would overflow the right edge. The wrapper-bottom check matters for short
editors (chat composers, compact embeds) where the popup would spill past the
editor's visible box even when the viewport has room below. The flipped state
adds the `inkwell-plugin-picker-popup-flipped` class to the popup root for any
flip-specific styling.
